metadata.name: Tohoku Earthquake and Tsunami
metadata.source: Japan Meteorological Agency / World Bank / OICA
metadata.description: A magnitude 9.0 undersea megathrust earthquake off the Pacific coast of Tohoku, Japan, triggered a devastating tsunami with waves up to 40m. The disaster caused roughly $235B in direct damage, crippled Japan's automotive and electronics supply chains for 6+ months, and exposed extreme concentration risk in single-source semiconductor and auto-parts suppliers located in the Tohoku and Kanto regions.

recovery_milestones.description: Partial port operations resumed at Sendai
Major automotive OEMs resume partial production

lessons_learned: Single-source dependency on Japan-based Tier-2 and Tier-3 suppliers created catastrophic hidden risk across automotive and electronics sectors.
Just-in-time inventory practices amplified disruption; companies with safety stock buffers recovered 40% faster.
Multi-tier supply chain mapping was nearly nonexistent pre-crisis; most OEMs had no visibility below Tier-1.
Nuclear meltdown at Fukushima Daiichi compounded logistics disruption with exclusion zones and power shortages.
Post-crisis, Toyota developed the RESCUE supply-chain risk database, mapping 400,000+ parts to geographic risk.
